examples.title: Basic Entity and Attribute Representation
examples.description: Demonstrates how to represent ER diagram entities and their attributes using Python classes, reflecting basic ER notation concepts
examples.code:
# ER Diagram Notation - Basic Entity Representation
# Representing Entity Sets and Attributes

class Entity:
    def __init__(self, name, attributes, primary_key):
        self.name = name
        self.attributes = attributes  # List of attribute names
        self.primary_key = primary_key  # Primary key attribute
    
    def __str__(self):
        return f"Entity: {self.name}\nAttributes: {self.attributes}\nPrimary Key: {self.primary_key}"

# Define entities for a University database
student = Entity(
    name="Student",
    attributes=["student_id", "name", "email", "phone", "enrollment_date"],
    primary_key="student_id"
)

course = Entity(
    name="Course",
    attributes=["course_id", "course_name", "credits", "department"],
    primary_key="course_id"
)

instructor = Entity(
    name="Instructor",
    attributes=["instructor_id", "name", "email", "specialization"],
    primary_key="instructor_id"
)

# Display entities
print("=== ER Diagram Entity Representation ===")
print(student)
print("\n" + "="*40)
print(course)
print("\n" + "="*40)
print(instructor)
examples.explanation: This example shows how to programmatically represent ER diagram components. Each Entity class stores the entity name, its attributes (both key and non-key), and identifies the primary key. In ER notation, entities are represented as rectangles, attributes as ovals, and primary keys are underlined.

examples.title: Relationship Sets with Cardinality
examples.description: Demonstrates ER diagram relationships (1:1, 1:N, M:N) with cardinality ratios using Python data structures
examples.code:
# ER Diagram Notation - Relationships and Cardinality
# Representing relationship sets with participation constraints

class Relationship:
    def __init__(self, name, entities, cardinality, participation):
        self.name = name
        self.entities = entities  # List of participating entities
        self.cardinality = cardinality  # 1:1, 1:N, or M:N
        self.participation = participation  # total or partial for each entity
    
    def __str__(self):
        result = f"Relationship: {self.name}\n"
        result += f"Participating Entities: {', '.join(self.entities)}\n"
        result += f"Cardinality: {self.cardinality}\n"
        result += f"Participation: {self.participation}"
        return result

# Define relationships for University database
# 1. Student enrolls in Course (M:N relationship)
enrolls = Relationship(
    name="Enrolls",
    entities=["Student", "Course"],
    cardinality="M:N",
    participation={"Student": "total", "Course": "total"}
)

# 2. Department has Instructors (1:N relationship)
has = Relationship(
    name="Has",
    entities=["Department", "Instructor"],
    cardinality="1:N",
    participation={"Department": "total", "Instructor": "partial"}
)

# 3. Course is taught by Instructor (1:1 or 1:N)
teaches = Relationship(
    name="Teaches",
    entities=["Course", "Instructor"],
    cardinality="1:1",
    participation={"Course": "total", "Instructor": "partial"}
)

# 4. Student has Advisor (1:1 relationship)
has_advisor = Relationship(
    name="Has_Advisor",
    entities=["Student", "Instructor"],
    cardinality="1:1",
    participation={"Student": "total", "Instructor": "partial"}
)

# Display relationships
print("=== ER Diagram Relationship Notation ===")
relationships = [enrolls, has, teaches, has_advisor]
for rel in relationships:
    print(rel)
    print("\n" + "-"*40)

# Visual notation guide
print("\n=== ER Relationship Notation Guide ===")
print("1:1  - One to One    (1 line on each side)")
print("1:N  - One to Many   (1 line, crow's foot on many side)")
print("M:N  - Many to Many  (crow's foot on both sides)")
print("Total participation (double line) - every entity must participate")
print("Partial participation (single line) - participation is optional")
examples.explanation: This example demonstrates relationship sets in ER diagrams. Relationships connect entities and have cardinality ratios (1:1, 1:N, M:N) and participation constraints (total/partial). In ER notation, relationships are represented as diamonds. The crow's foot notation is used to show 'many' side of relationships.

examples.title: ER to Relational Schema Conversion
examples.description: Advanced example showing conversion of ER diagram notation to relational database schema, including weak entities and derived attributes
examples.code:
# ER Diagram Notation - Converting ER Model to Relational Schema
# Implementation of conversion rules for different entity types

def convert_strong_entity(entity_name, attributes, primary_key):
    """Convert strong entity to relation (table)"""
    schema = f"CREATE TABLE {entity_name} (\n"
    for attr in attributes:
        if attr == primary_key:
            schema += f"    {attr} PRIMARY KEY,\n"
        else:
            schema += f"    {attr},\n"
    schema = schema.rstrip(",\n") + "\n);"
    return schema

def convert_weak_entity(entity_name, attributes, primary_key, owner_entity):
    """Convert weak entity - includes owner entity's primary key"""
    schema = f"CREATE TABLE {entity_name} (\n"
    # Add owner primary key as foreign key
    schema += f"    {owner_entity.lower()}_{primary_key} REFERENCES {owner_entity}({primary_key}),\n"
    schema += f"    {primary_key},\n"  # Partial key
    for attr in attributes:
        if attr != primary_key:
            schema += f"    {attr},\n"
    # Composite primary key
    schema += f"    PRIMARY KEY ({owner_entity.lower()}_{primary_key}, {primary_key})\n"
    schema += ");"
    return schema

def convert_binary_relationship(rel_name, entity1, entity2, cardinality, participation):
    """Convert binary relationship to relation or foreign key"""
    if cardinality == "1:1":
        # Add foreign key to either side based on participation
        if participation[entity1] == "total":
            return f"-- Add {entity2}_id as FK in {entity1} (total participation)"
        else:
            return f"-- Add {entity1}_id as FK in {entity2}"
    elif cardinality == "1:N":
        # Add foreign key on 'many' side
        return f"-- Add {entity1.lower()}_id as FK in {entity2}"
    elif cardinality == "M:N":
        # Create new relation for many-to-many
        schema = f"CREATE TABLE {rel_name} (\n"
        schema += f"    {entity1.lower()}_id,\n"
        schema += f"    {entity2.lower()}_id,\n"
        schema += f"    PRIMARY KEY ({entity1.lower()}_id, {entity2.lower()}_id),\n"
        schema += f"    FOREIGN KEY ({entity1.lower()}_id) REFERENCES {entity1}(id),\n"
        schema += f"    FOREIGN KEY ({entity2.lower()}_id) REFERENCES {entity2}(id)\n"
        schema += ");"
        return schema
    return ""

# Example: University ER Diagram to Schema
print("=== ER to Relational Schema Conversion ===\n")

# 1. Strong Entity: Student
print("1. STRONG ENTITY: Student")
print(convert_strong_entity("Student", ["student_id", "name", "email", "dob"], "student_id"))
print()

# 2. Weak Entity: Dependent (weak on Employee)
print("2. WEAK ENTITY: Dependent (weak on Employee)")
print(convert_weak_entity("Dependent", ["dependent_id", "name", "relationship"], "dependent_id", "Employee"))
print()

# 3. M:N Relationship: Enrolls (Student-Course)
print("3. M:N RELATIONSHIP: Enrolls")
print(convert_binary_relationship("Enrolls", "Student", "Course", "M:N", {"Student": "total", "Course": "total"}))
print()

# 4. 1:N Relationship: Department-Instructor
print("4. 1:N RELATIONSHIP: Department-Has-Instructor")
print(convert_binary_relationship("Has", "Department", "Instructor", "1:N", {"Department": "total", "Instructor": "partial"}))

print("\n=== ER Notation Summary ===")
print("Strong Entity: Rectangle (single line)")
print("Weak Entity: Rectangle (double line)")
print("Attribute: Oval (derived: dashed)")
print("Relationship: Diamond (double diamond for identifying)")
print("Primary Key: Underlined attribute")
print("Total Participation: Double line on entity side")
print("Partial Participation: Single line on entity side")
examples.explanation: This advanced example demonstrates how to convert ER diagram notation to actual relational database schema. It covers conversion rules for: (1) Strong entities become tables with primary keys, (2) Weak entities include owner entity's primary key as foreign key with composite key, (3) 1:1 relationships use foreign key in either direction, (4) 1:N relationships add foreign key to the 'many' side, (5) M:N relationships create a separate junction table. This is essential for practical database design.
